But when it comes to science, despite its importance not showing up at church services or casual chats around Bari cafes much, there's a clear gap where women are underrepresented.\n\nI think addressing this is crucial because science needs all kinds of minds working together—like my grandson who loves building things but sometimes gets discouraged by the lack of female role models in technical fields. We should start early with education programs that encourage girls right from primary school to explore STEM subjects without any bias, maybe through hands-on activities like fixing bicycles or electrical circuits which we do at home anyway.\n\nAlso, companies need better support systems for women returning after family breaks—being a widow means I handle everything myself now; imagine juggling work and raising four kids alone! response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
As a person with limited formal education but plenty of life experience, I've seen firsthand how opportunities are unevenly distributed. In my work as an electrician and living through various changes, like becoming widowed after my husband passed away during our time in Rome for his business, I understand that sticking to the same routine can be tough. But when it comes to science, despite its importance not showing up at church services or casual chats around Bari cafes much, there's a clear gap where women are underrepresented.

I think addressing this is crucial because science needs all kinds of minds working together—like my grandson who loves building things but sometimes gets discouraged by the lack of female role models in technical fields. We should start early with education programs that encourage girls right from primary school to explore STEM subjects without any bias, maybe through hands-on activities like fixing bicycles or electrical circuits which we do at home anyway.

Also, companies need better support systems for women returning after family breaks—being a widow means I handle everything myself now; imagine juggling work and raising four kids alone! Having mentorship networks could help bridge the gap without being too pushy about social issues.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I analyzed my own background as an electrician with limited education to infer that practical skills might be underestimated in scientific contexts, but I know from life experience that opportunities aren't fair. Considering my city's context and the fact that women are often left out of technical fields, I proposed starting early with inclusive STEM programs because exposure matters regardless of formal schooling level.

I also thought about support systems for work-life balance since being widowed affects how we manage careers—maybe companies could implement better parental leave policies including men to encourage more female participation from the beginning. My high openness score suggests curiosity towards new ideas, so I believe in exploring these solutions without forcing them.
citations: No formal citations required as it's based on life experience
